chunk_summary: This section addresses troubleshooting fortification of expressed and donor milk in NICU settings, focusing on preterm infants' nutritional needs and metabolic challenges.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of human milk fortification in meeting preterm infants' nutritional needs.
Identify strategies to manage metabolic bone disease in premature infants.
Evaluate the impact of fortifiers on human milk osmolarity and its clinical implications.
Explore guidelines for donor milk banking and fortification in NICU settings.
key_facts: Human milk fortification is essential for preterm infants to meet protein and mineral requirements.
Metabolic bone disease affects up to 50% of very low birth weight infants, requiring careful calcium and phosphorus supplementation.
Fortifiers can increase human milk osmolarity by up to 20%, which may impact gastrointestinal tolerance.
Donor milk is often fortified to match the nutritional needs of preterm infants, as it may lack sufficient protein compared to maternal milk.
European Milk Bank Association recommends individualized fortification protocols for NICU cases.
reference_quote: Fortification of human milk for preterm infants is critical to address their unique nutritional needs, as outlined by the European Milk Bank Association Working Group (PMC6439523).
document_key_topics: Human milk fortification for preterm infants
Metabolic bone disease in premature infants
Impact of fortifiers on milk osmolarity
